title: Carbon and Its Compounds
subject: science
audioScript: Good morning, everyone. Today we're opening Carbon and Its Compounds. Forget the heavy textbook tone for a moment — think of this chapter as a story about Covalent bonding, Homologous series, and Functional groups. I'll use everyday examples first, then we'll connect them to what CBSE expects in the exam.

Forget the heavy textbook tone for a moment — think of this chapter as a story about Covalent bonding, Homologous series, and Functional groups. Imagine I'm sitting beside you before the board exam. Here's Carbon and Its Compounds the way I'd explain it to a student who said 'I read it but didn't get it.' Slow pace, simple words, one idea at a time.
Quick recap of Carbon and Its Compounds. Three things to remember tonight: the big idea, one formula or rule, and one mistake to avoid. That's enough for a confident revision.

sections.title: 1. Concept Overview
sections.body:
Facts about Carbon:
□Carbon is the 15th most abundant element in the earth's crust.
□ Earth's crust has 0.02% carbon in form of minerals like carbonates, hydrogen carbonates, coal & petroleum.
□ Carbon is present in atmosphere primarily in the form of CO₂. Its concentration is 0.03%. Also traces of CO are found.
□ The shells of aquatic animals are made from calcium carbonate.
Give a Thought:
□ Most of the things that we use daily are made up of compounds of carbon like – books, clothes, food, etc.
□ To confirm this: On burning carbon-containing compounds, a colourless & odourless gas called CO₂ is produced.
Chemical Equation:
C + O₂ CO₂
→
Confirmation Test of CO₂
(Limewater) (Calcium Carbonate)
Ca(OH)₂ = Calcium Hydroxide CaCO₃ = Calcium Carbonate
Observation:
CO₂ turns limewater turbid/milky.
Reason:
The milkiness is due to the formation of insoluble calcium carbonate (CaCO₃).
3.
Bonding in Carbon– The Covalent Bond
The bonds formed by the sharing of an electron pair between atoms are known as covalent bonds.
1. Monovalency – Hydrogen (H₂)
Hydrogen atom shares 1 electron.
H : H
•Each H-atom shares 1 electron.
•Forms a single covalent bond between two hydrogen atoms.
2. Divalency –…

Forget the heavy textbook tone for a moment — think of this chapter as a story about Covalent bonding, Homologous series, and Functional groups. I'll use everyday examples first, then we'll connect them to what CBSE expects in the exam.

sections.title: 2. Key Definitions
sections.body:
**Key definitions:**

- □Carbon is the 15th most abundant element in the earth's crust.
- □ Carbon is present in atmosphere primarily in the form of CO₂. Its concentration is 0.03%. Also traces of CO are found.
- □ The shells of aquatic animals are made from calcium carbonate.
- □ Most of the things that we use daily are made up of compounds of carbon like – books, clothes, food, etc.
- □ To confirm this: On burning carbon-containing compounds, a colourless & odourless gas called CO₂ is produced.
- The milkiness is due to the formation of insoluble calcium carbonate (CaCO₃).
- The bonds formed by the sharing of an electron pair between atoms are known as covalent bonds.
- C⁴⁺ is unstable as it has 6 protons in its nucleus holding only 2 electrons.

sections.title: 3. Core Concepts
sections.body:
Facts about Carbon:
□Carbon is the 15th most abundant element in the earth's crust.
□ Earth's crust has 0.02% carbon in form of minerals like carbonates, hydrogen carbonates, coal & petroleum.
□ Carbon is present in atmosphere primarily in the form of CO₂. Its concentration is 0.03%. Also traces of CO are found.
□ The shells of aquatic animals are made from calcium carbonate.
Give a Thought:
□ Most of the things that we use daily are made up of compounds of carbon like – books, clothes, food, etc.
□ To confirm this: On burning carbon-containing compounds, a colourless & odourless gas called CO₂ is produced.
Chemical Equation:
C + O₂ CO₂
→
Confirmation Test of CO₂
(Limewater) (Calcium Carbonate)
Ca(OH)₂ = Calcium Hydroxide CaCO₃ = Calcium Carbonate
Observation:
CO₂ turns limewater turbid/milky.
Reason:
The milkiness is due to the formation of insoluble calcium carbonate (CaCO₃).
3.
Bonding in Carbon– The Covalent Bond
The bonds formed by the sharing of an electron pair between atoms are known as covalent bonds.
1. Monovalency – Hydrogen (H₂)
Hydrogen atom shares 1 electron.
H : H
•Each H-atom shares 1 electron.
•Forms a single covalent bond between two hydrogen atoms.
2. Divalency – Oxygen (O₂)
Oxygen atom shares 2 electrons.
O :: O
•Each O-atom shares 2 electrons.
•Forms a double covalent bond between two oxygen atoms.
3. Trivalency – Nitrogen (N₂)
Nitrogen atom shares 3 electrons.
N N
≡
Each N-atom shares 3 electrons.
Forms a triple covalent bond between two nitrogen atoms.
4.
Valency Example ElectronsShared Bond Type
Monovalency Hydrogen (H₂) 1 Single Covalent Bond
Divalency Oxygen (O₂) 2 Double Covalent Bond
Trivalency Nitrogen (N₂) 3 Triple Covalent Bond
Summary Table
Case of Carbon
•Carbon has 4 electrons in its outermost shell.
Carbon cannot gain 4 electrons
•Due to the small size of the carbon atom, 6 protons of carbon cannot hold 10 electrons.
•Repulsion between electrons increases.
Carbon cannot lose 4 electrons
•It would require a large amount of energy to…

sections.title: 4. Formulas / Theorems
sections.body:
**Formulas and relations:**

- Ca(OH)₂ = Calcium Hydroxide CaCO₃ = Calcium Carbonate
- In CH3Cl : C = 6, H = 1 and Cl = 17 And their electronic configuration is C – 2,4, H – 1 and Cl – 2, 8, 7
- For butanol, n = 4. So, formula is
- (d) Propene, CH3CH=CH2 (ii) and propyne, CH3— C = CH (iii) both have double and triple bonds, respectively, hence are unsaturated
- (b) Electronic configuration of carbon (C) = 2, 4 when it forms four covalent bonds by sharing its four valence electrons wi
- Atomic number of carbon = 6
- E.g. CH2 = CH2 + H2 + (Nickel catalyst) → CH3– CH3

sections.title: 5. Solved Examples
sections.body:
**Worked examples:**

- Valency Example ElectronsShared Bond Type
- For example, acidic K2Cr2O7 is an oxidising agent, that converts (oxidises) ethanol into ethanoic acid.
- What is a homologous series ? Explain with an example.
- (i) All members of a homologous series can be represented by the same general formula. For example, the general formula of the homologous series of alkanes is CnH2n+2, in which ‘n’ denotes number of carbon and hydrogen atoms in one molecule of alkane.
- For example, general formula of the homologous series of alkanes is CnH2n+2, in which ‘n’ denotes number of carbon atoms in one molecule of alkane. Following are the first five members of the homologous series of alkanes (general formula CnH2n+2).
- What is a homologous series? Explain with an example.

sections.title: 8. Practice Questions
sections.body:
_Class 10 CBSE practice for **Carbon and Its Compounds**._

**Practice questions:**

1. 3. Bonding in Carbon– The Covalent Bond
2. 1. Monovalency – Hydrogen (H₂)
3. 2. Divalency – Oxygen (O₂)
4. 3. Trivalency – Nitrogen (N₂)
5. 4. Valency Example ElectronsShared Bond Type
6. 5. Properties of CovalentCompounds
7. 1. Made from Molecules
8. 3. Electrical Conductivity
9. 5. Low Melting & Boiling Point
10. 7. Allotropes of Carbon

**More practice**

- **SHORT (2 marks)** — Explain **Covalent bonding** in 2–3 sentences. Use one example from Carbon and Its Compounds.
- **MCQ (1 mark)** — MCQ: Which statement about **Covalent bonding** is most accurate (Class 10 CBSE)? Review your notes, then justify your choice.
- **SHORT (2 marks)** — Explain **Homologous series** in 2–3 sentences. Use one example from Carbon and Its Compounds.

sections.title: 11. Video Transcript Summary
sections.body:
_Teacher recap — what a strong Class 10 video lesson on **Carbon and Its Compounds** should cover:_

**Lesson flow (as if narrated on screen):**

1. **Hook** — Start with a daily-life example tied to Carbon and Its Compounds, not a textbook definition.
2. **Covalent bonding** — Show a labelled diagram or quick demo, then state the CBSE definition in one sentence.
3. **Homologous series** — Show a labelled diagram or quick demo, then state the CBSE definition in one sentence.
4. **Functional groups** — Show a labelled diagram or quick demo, then state the CBSE definition in one sentence.
5. **Soaps and detergents** — Show a labelled diagram or quick demo, then state the CBSE definition in one sentence.
6. **Common mistake** — Warn about the error students make most often in exams.
7. **Recap** — Three bullet points to remember before moving to practice questions.

**After watching, you should be able to:**

- Explain Covalent bonding in everyday language.
- Draw or describe one diagram related to Carbon and Its Compounds.
- Attempt at least two short-answer questions without notes.

**Videos linked for this chapter:**

- 🔍 [CBSE class 10 Carbon and Its Compounds NCERT explained Hindi English](https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=CBSE+class+10+Carbon+and+Its+Compounds+NCERT+explained+Hindi+English)
- 🔍 [Carbon and Its Compounds class 10 one shot revision](https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=Carbon+and+Its+Compounds+class+10+one+shot+revision)
- 🔍 [NCERT Science class 10 Carbon and Its Compounds animation](https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=NCERT+Science+class+10+Carbon+and+Its+Compounds+animation)
